subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11! You already know your way around small multiplication and division facts, and in this unit you will grow those skills into something much more powerful. You will learn to multiply and divide by 10 and 100 in a flash, to break big multiplications apart with the grid method, to handle division that does not come out evenly by using remainders, and to choose the right operation when a problem is dressed up as a real-life story.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Multiply whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain the place-value pattern behind it.
Divide whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain why the digits move to the right.
Use the grid method to multiply a two-digit number by a one-digit number.
Use the grid method to multiply a two-digit number by a two-digit number.
Carry out division that leaves a remainder, and write the answer correctly (for example 47 ÷ 6 = 7 r 5).
Use your multiplication facts to work out division facts quickly.
Interpret a remainder in a real-world story – deciding whether to round up, round down, or share it out.
Read a word problem, choose multiplication or division, solve it, and check that the answer makes sense.

concepts.title: Multiplying by 10 and 100
concepts.explanation: Multiplying by 10 is one of the most useful shortcuts in all of mathematics, and there is a beautiful pattern behind it. Look at these examples: Multiplying by 100 follows exactly the same idea, only bigger. Look at these:
concepts.example: When we multiply by 100, every digit moves two places to the left, and we write two zeros to hold the ones and tens places empty. In 5 × 100, the 5 jumps all the way from the ones place to the hundreds place. In 32 ×…

concepts.title: Dividing by 10 and 100
concepts.explanation: Division by 10 is the opposite, or inverse, of multiplication by 10. If multiplying by 10 slides every digit one place to the left, then dividing by 10 slides every digit one place to the right. Look at these examples: Dividing by 100 moves every digit two places to the right, and removes two zeros from a number that ends in two zeros. Look at these:
concepts.example: This is extremely handy with money. If 10 notebooks cost 45 dollars, then one notebook costs 45 ÷ 10 = 4 dollars 50 cents. If 100 stickers cost 350 dollars, then one sticker costs 350 ÷ 100 = 3 dollars 50 cents. Because…

concepts.title: The Grid Method for Multiplication
concepts.explanation: When numbers get bigger, multiplying them in your head becomes hard. The grid method (sometimes called the box method) makes it easy again by breaking a big multiplication into small, friendly pieces. The idea rests on place value: you split each number into its tens and ones, multiply the parts separately, then add the pieces back together. Let us multiply 34 × 6 step by step. First, split 34 into 30 and 4. Draw a grid with one row and two columns, and write 30 and 4 along the top, with 6 down the side:
concepts.example: Now add the two partial products: 180 + 24 = 204. So 34 × 6 = 204. Notice how we turned one hard multiplication into two easy ones. Multiplying 30 × 6 is really just 3 × 6 = 18 with a zero added, and 4 × 6 = 24 is a…

concepts.title: Division with Remainders
concepts.explanation: When you divide, the numbers do not always fit together evenly. The amount left over is called the remainder. Understanding remainders is one of the most important ideas in this unit, because in real life things very often do not share out perfectly. Let us look at 17 ÷ 5. How many whole groups of 5 fit inside 17? We can fit 3 groups, because 5 × 3 = 15. That uses up 15, and 17 − 15 = 2 are left over. So we write 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2, which is often shortened to 3 r 2. The 3 is the quotient (the number of whole…
concepts.example: Here is a careful step-by-step method using 47 ÷ 6:

concepts.title: Interpreting Remainders in Real Life
concepts.explanation: A remainder is not just a number at the end of a sum – in a real problem it means something, and what you do with it depends on the story. This is where many learners slip, so read this concept carefully. Imagine 29 students need to be put into teams of 5. We work out 29 ÷ 5 = 5 r 4. We can make 5 complete teams (that uses 25 students) and 4 students are left over. What should happen to those 4? Depending on the situation you might: add them to existing teams to make some teams a little larger, form one smaller…
concepts.example: Sometimes the remainder tells you to round up. If 156 students are going on a trip to Mombasa and each bus carries 52 students, then 156 ÷ 52 = 3 exactly, so 3 buses are enough. But if there were 160 students, 160 ÷ 52…

concepts.title: Multiplication and Division Are Inverses
concepts.explanation: Multiplication and division are like two sides of the same coin – each one undoes the other. This idea is called being inverse operations, and it is one of the most useful tools you have. Every multiplication fact hides a pair of division facts inside it. These four related facts are called a fact family. Take the fact 6 × 7 = 42. From this single fact we instantly know four things:
concepts.example: 7 × 6 = 42 (the order of the factors does not change the product – this is the commutative property)

methods.title: The grid method (two-digit by one-digit)
steps: Split the two-digit number into tens and ones (34 becomes 30 and 4).
Multiply each part by the one-digit number (30 × 6 = 180 and 4 × 6 = 24).
Add the partial products (180 + 24 = 204).

methods.title: The grid method (two-digit by two-digit)
steps: Split both numbers into tens and ones (26 → 20 and 6; 38 → 30 and 8).
Fill all four boxes: 20 × 30, 6 × 30, 20 × 8, 6 × 8.
Add the four partial products together (600 + 180 + 160 + 48 = 988).

methods.title: Division with a remainder
methods.example: Use the grid method to calculate 27 × 5.
steps: Find the largest multiple of the divisor that fits inside the dividend.
Subtract it to find what is left over – that is the remainder.
Write the answer as a whole number with the remainder (47 ÷ 6 = 7 r 5).
Check: the remainder must be smaller than the divisor.

methods.title: Grid method, two-digit by two-digit
methods.example: Use the grid method to calculate 32 × 24.
steps: Solution: Split 32 into 30 and 2, and 24 into 20 and 4.
The four partial products are: 30 × 20 = 600, 2 × 20 = 40, 30 × 4 = 120, and 2 × 4 = 8.

methods.title: Division with a remainder
steps: Solution: Look for the largest multiple of 9 that fits inside 64.
Since 9 × 7 = 63 (fits) and 9 × 8 = 72 (too big), 7 groups fit.
The remainder is 64 − 63 = 1.

methods.title: A remainder you must round up
methods.example: A baker has 53 cookies and packs them into boxes of 8. How many full boxes can she make, and how many cookies are left over?
steps: Solution: 53 ÷ 8.
Since 8 × 6 = 48 (fits) and 8 × 7 = 56 (too big), 6 boxes are full and 53 − 48 = 5 cookies are left over.
So she makes 6 full boxes with 5 cookies left over.
(If the question had asked how many boxes she needs to hold every cookie, she would need a 7th box for those 5.)

workedExamples.title: Multiplying by 10 and 100
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 18 × 10, slide every digit one place left and add a zero: 18 × 10 = 180. For 25 × 100, slide every digit two places left and add two zeros: 25 × 100 = 2,500.

workedExamples.title: Dividing by 10 and 100
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 420 ÷ 10, slide every digit one place right, removing one zero: 420 ÷ 10 = 42. For 600 ÷ 100, slide every digit two places right, removing two zeros: 600 ÷ 100 = 6.

workedExamples.title: Grid method, two-digit by one-digit
workedExamples.prompt: Use the grid method to calculate 27 × 5.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Split 27 into 20 and 7. Then 20 × 5 = 100 and 7 × 5 = 35. Add the partial products: 100 + 35 = 135. So 27 × 5 = 135.

workedExamples.title: Grid method, two-digit by two-digit
workedExamples.prompt: Use the grid method to calculate 32 × 24.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Split 32 into 30 and 2, and 24 into 20 and 4. The four partial products are: 30 × 20 = 600, 2 × 20 = 40, 30 × 4 = 120, and 2 × 4 = 8. Add them: 600 + 40 + 120 + 8 = 768. So 32 × 24 = 768.

workedExamples.title: Division with a remainder
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Look for the largest multiple of 9 that fits inside 64. Since 9 × 7 = 63 (fits) and 9 × 8 = 72 (too big), 7 groups fit. The remainder is 64 − 63 = 1. So 64 ÷ 9 = 7 r 1. Check: the remainder 1 is smaller than the divisor 9, so this is correct.

workedExamples.title: A remainder you must round up
workedExamples.prompt: A baker has 53 cookies and packs them into boxes of 8. How many full boxes can she make, and how many cookies are left over?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 53 ÷ 8. Since 8 × 6 = 48 (fits) and 8 × 7 = 56 (too big), 6 boxes are full and 53 − 48 = 5 cookies are left over. So she makes 6 full boxes with 5 cookies left over. (If the question had asked how many boxes she needs to hold every cookie, she would need a 7th box for those 5.)

workedExamples.title: Using a fact family to divide
workedExamples.prompt: A teacher has 42 pencils to share equally among 6 students. How many pencils does each student get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This is 42 ÷ 6. Ask, what number times 6 makes 42? Since 6 × 7 = 42, the answer is 42 ÷ 6 = 7 pencils each. There is no remainder, because 42 is an exact multiple of 6.

workedExamples.title: Choosing the operation in a word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A baker uses 3 eggs to make one batch of cookies. How many eggs are needed for 8 batches?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The word each and the idea of repeating equal groups tell us to multiply: 8 batches × 3 eggs = 8 × 3 = 24 eggs. Check that it makes sense: more batches need more eggs, and 24 is bigger than 3, so the answer is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A teacher shares 96 pencils equally among 8 students. How many pencils each?

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Use a related multiplication fact to work out 63 ÷ 7, and check it by multiplying back.

workedExamples.prompt: Use the grid method: a) 46 × 23 b) 54 × 32.

activities.title: The Zero Slide (Multiply and Divide by 10 and 100)
activities.materials: number cards 0 to 9 and a place-value chart with columns for hundreds, tens and ones (draw one on paper).
steps: Place a two-digit number on the chart, for example 45.
Show × 10 by sliding every digit one column to the left and writing a zero in the ones column.
Show × 100 by sliding every digit two columns to the left and writing two zeros.
Now reverse it: slide the digits back to the right to show ÷ 10 and ÷ 100. Say what you notice about the zeros.
Watching the digits slide helps you see that multiplying and dividing by 10 and 100 is all about place value, not just adding zeros.

activities.title: Build a Grid (Box Method with Household Objects)
activities.materials: grid paper or a hand-drawn box, a pencil.
steps: Choose a two-digit by one-digit multiplication, such as 34 × 6.
Draw a grid and split 34 into 30 and 4 along the top, with 6 down the side.
Fill each box: 30 × 6 = 180 and 4 × 6 = 24.
Add the partial products to find the answer, then check it a second way (for example by repeated addition of 34).
Try harder ones next, like 26 × 38, and see how the grid keeps all four partial products tidy.

activities.title: Division Detective (Remainders with Counters)
activities.materials: a handful of small objects – dry beans, stones, or bottle caps.
steps: Count out 23 objects.
Arrange them into equal groups of 4. How many complete groups can you make?
Count how many objects are left over.
Write the division sentence: 23 ÷ 4 = 5 r 3. Then try new numbers of objects and new group sizes.
Seeing the leftover objects with your own eyes makes the meaning of a remainder crystal clear.

activities.title: Market Multiplication (Family Task)
activities.materials: a real or pretend market, with stones standing in for shillings and household items for goods.
steps: Set a price for one item, for example 12 shillings for a bag of dates.
A family member asks: How much for 10 bags? For 100 bags? Use your place-value slide to answer.
Now share out goods: 47 dates into bags of 6. How many full bags, and how many dates are left?
Talk about what to do with the leftover dates in real life.

activities.title: Fact Family Fold (Multiplication and Division Game)
steps: Pick three numbers that make a fact, such as 6, 7 and 42.
Write all four members of the fact family: 6 × 7 = 42, 7 × 6 = 42, 42 ÷ 6 = 7, 42 ÷ 7 = 6.
Fold the paper to hide the answer to one fact and challenge a partner to say it.
Make ten different fact-family cards and quiz each other to build speed.
This game shows you that every multiplication fact you know gives you a division fact for free.

activities.title: Remainder Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a die or number cards.
steps: Roll or pick a two-digit number to be the dividend.
Divide it by 5 and record the remainder. Repeat for many different numbers.
List all the remainders you get. Which remainders are possible when you divide by 5? (You should never see 5 or more.)
Now try dividing by 4 and by 6, and describe the pattern in the possible remainders.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the grid method or remainders best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Multiplying by 10 and 100 × 10: every digit moves one place to the left; add one zero. 24 × 10 = 240. × 100: every digit moves two places to the left; add two zeros. 24 × 100 = 2,400.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Dividing by 10 and 100 ÷ 10: every digit moves one place to the right; remove one zero. 240 ÷ 10 = 24. ÷ 100: every digit moves two places to the right; remove two zeros. 2,400 ÷ 100 = 24.

rules.title: Remainder Rule
rules.text: The remainder is what is left over after making as many equal groups as possible. The remainder is always smaller than the divisor.

terms: The amount left over after dividing
One part of a grid-method answer
The four related × and ÷ facts from three numbers
The opposite operation that undoes another
commonMistakes: × 100 shifts two places → add two zeros
Remainder must be smaller than the divisor
Round up, round down, or share, to fit the story

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Use the grid method: a) 46 × 23 b) 54 × 32.
A theatre has 36 rows with 25 seats in each row. How many seats in total? Use the grid method.
89 apples are packed into bags of 10. How many full bags, and how many left over?
Explain, with an example, why the remainder in a division can never be as large as the divisor.

Explain the key idea in Multiplication and Division Are Inverses.

questions.question: Use the grid method to calculate 27 × 5.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Split 27 into 20 and 7. Then 20 × 5 = 100 and 7 × 5 = 35. Add the partial products: 100 + 35 = 135. So 27 × 5 = 135.

questions.question: Use the grid method to calculate 32 × 24.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Split 32 into 30 and 2, and 24 into 20 and 4. The four partial products are: 30 × 20 = 600, 2 × 20 = 40, 30 × 4 = 120, and 2 × 4 = 8. Add them: 600 + 40 + 120 + 8 = 768. So 32 × 24 = 768.

Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Product means The answer to a multiplication.

rounds.prompt: Use the grid method to calculate 27 × 5.
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Split 27 into 20 and 7. Then 20 × 5 = 100 and 7 × 5 = 35. Add the partial products: 100 + 35 = 135. So 27 × 5 = 135.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

choices: The amount left over after dividing
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Quotient means The answer to a division.

rounds.prompt: Use the grid method to calculate 32 × 24.
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
rounds.explanation: Split 32 into 30 and 2, and 24 into 20 and 4. The four partial products are: 30 × 20 = 600, 2 × 20 = 40, 30 × 4 = 120, and 2 × 4 = 8. Add them: 600 + 40 + 120 + 8 = 768. So 32 × 24 = 768.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

selfAssessment: I can multiply whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain the place-value pattern behind it.
I can divide whole numbers by 10 and 100 and explain why the digits move to the right.
I can use the grid method to multiply a two-digit number by a one-digit number.
I can use the grid method to multiply a two-digit number by a two-digit number.
I can carry out division that leaves a remainder, and write the answer correctly (for example 47 ÷ 6 = 7 r 5).
I can use your multiplication facts to work out division facts quickly.
I can interpret a remainder in a real-world story – deciding whether to round up, round down, or share it out.
I can read a word problem, choose multiplication or division, solve it, and check that the answer makes sense.
